[[Matsumae Domain]] trader and agent {{ill|Murayama Denbee|ja|村山伝兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Denbee|qq=}} (1738–1813) is said to have founded the small shrine of {{nihongo|Sōya Daijingū|宗谷大神宮}} In [[:en:Tenmei|Tenmei]] 5 (1785), enshrining [[Amaterasu]] as guardian of the north gate.<ref name="Jinjacho">{{cite web |url=https://hokkaidojinjacho.jp/北門神社/ |script-title=ja:北門神社 |trans-title=Hokumon Jinja |language=Japanese |publisher=Hokkaidō Jinja-chō |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.city.wakkanai.hokkaido.jp/gaiyo/ayumi.html |script-title=ja:稚内のあゆみ（歴史）|trans-title=History of Wakkanai |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Wakkanai, Hokkaido|Wakkanai City]] |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref> In 1896 the shrine was transferred to its current location and renamed [[Hokumon Shrine]], with [[Takemikazuchi]] and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] enshrined alongside Amaterasu.<ref name="Jinjacho"/>

==Overview==
'''Murayama Denbē''' (村山 伝兵衛) was a merchant who expanded from [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]] to [[:en:Ezo|Ezo]] during the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku era]]. The name "Denbē" was passed down through successive heads of the Murayama family.

The Murayama family's business was known as '''Abe-ya''' (安部屋 or 阿部屋), and their shop emblem was '''Marujūgo''' (○に十五), symbolizing the first Denbē's ambition to own 15 ships.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

== Successive Heads of the Murayama Family ==

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in [[:en:Hakui District, Ishikawa|Hakui District]], Noto Province, he moved to [[:en:Matsumae Domain|Matsumae Domain]] in his late teens or early twenties. He initially stayed at the home of a Matsumae retainer, Kudō Hachirōemon, while starting his business.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}} Some records indicate he may have worked as a servant for Harue Jinbē, who had already established Abe-ya in Matsumae.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in [[:en:Sōya District, Hokkaido|Sōya District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Tomamae District, Hokkaido|Tomamae District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Rumoi District, Hokkaido|Rumoi District, Hokkaido]], and [[:en:Ishikari District, Hokkaido|Ishikari District, Hokkaido]] under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu]].{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: [[:en:1716|1716]] ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – [[:en:1770|1770]] ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
: The son of Shimazaki Hanbē from [[:en:Tsuruga Port|Tsuruga Port]]. He worked for the Matsumae branch of the Omi merchant Manya Kiemon before being adopted as Denbē's son-in-law.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: [[:en:1738|1738]] ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa era]], he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By [[:en:1775|1775]] ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in [[:en:1778|1778]] ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the [[:en:Mashike District, Hokkaido|Mashike District, Hokkaido]] trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and [[:en:Machi-bugyō|Machi bugyō]] assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In [[:en:1789|1789]] ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the [[:en:Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion]], the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In [[:en:1790|1790]] ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in [[:en:Sakhalin|Sakhalin]], and by [[:en:1798|1798]] ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in [[:en:1796|1796]] ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in [[:en:1799|1799]] ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In [[:en:1915|1915]] ([[:en:1915|Taisho 3]]), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Legacy ==

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in [[:en:Minami-ku, Sapporo|Minami-ku, Sapporo]] was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on [[:en:Japan National Route 230|National Route 230 (Japan)]].{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu]].{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: [[:en:1716|1716]] ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – [[:en:1770|1770]] ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
: The son of Shimazaki Hanbē from [[:en:Tsuruga Port|Tsuruga Port]]. He worked for the Matsumae branch of the Omi merchant Manya Kiemon before being adopted as Denbē's son-in-law.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: [[:en:1738|1738]] ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa era]], he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By [[:en:1775|1775]] ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in [[:en:1778|1778]] ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the [[:en:Mashike District, Hokkaido|Mashike District, Hokkaido]] trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and [[:en:Machi-bugyō|Machi bugyō]] assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In [[:en:1789|1789]] ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the [[:en:Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion]], the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In [[:en:1790|1790]] ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in [[:en:Sakhalin|Sakhalin]], and by [[:en:1798|1798]] ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in [[:en:1796|1796]] ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in [[:en:1799|1799]] ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In [[:en:1915|1915]] ([[:en:1915|Taisho 3]]), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at [[:en:Hokkaidō Shrine|Hokkaido Shrine]]'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in [[:en:Minami-ku, Sapporo|Minami-ku, Sapporo]] was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on [[:en:Japan National Route 230|National Route 230 (Japan)]].{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

[[Matsumae Domain]] trader and agent {{ill|Murayama Denbee|ja|村山伝兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Denbee|qq=}} (1738–1813) is said to have founded the small shrine of {{nihongo|Sōya Daijingū|宗谷大神宮}} In [[:en:Tenmei|Tenmei]] 5 (1785), enshrining [[Amaterasu]] as guardian of the north gate.<ref name="Jinjacho">{{cite web |url=https://hokkaidojinjacho.jp/北門神社/ |script-title=ja:北門神社 |trans-title=Hokumon Jinja |language=Japanese |publisher=Hokkaidō Jinja-chō |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.city.wakkanai.hokkaido.jp/gaiyo/ayumi.html |script-title=ja:稚内のあゆみ（歴史）|trans-title=History of Wakkanai |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Wakkanai, Hokkaido|Wakkanai City]] |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref> In 1896 the shrine was transferred to its current location and renamed [[Hokumon Shrine]], with [[Takemikazuchi]] and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] enshrined alongside Amaterasu.<ref name="Jinjacho"/>

==Overview==
'''Murayama Denbē''' (村山 伝兵衛) was a merchant who expanded from [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]] to [[:en:Ezo|Ezo]] during the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku era]]. The name "Denbē" was passed down through successive heads of the Murayama family.

The Murayama family's business was known as '''Abe-ya''' (安部屋 or 阿部屋), and their shop emblem was '''Marujūgo''' (○に十五), symbolizing the first Denbē's ambition to own 15 ships.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

== Successive Heads of the Murayama Family ==

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in [[:en:Hakui District, Ishikawa|Hakui District]], Noto Province, he moved to [[:en:Matsumae Domain|Matsumae Domain]] in his late teens or early twenties. He initially stayed at the home of a Matsumae retainer, Kudō Hachirōemon, while starting his business.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}} Some records indicate he may have worked as a servant for Harue Jinbē, who had already established Abe-ya in Matsumae.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in [[:en:Sōya District, Hokkaido|Sōya District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Tomamae District, Hokkaido|Tomamae District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Rumoi District, Hokkaido|Rumoi District, Hokkaido]], and [[:en:Ishikari District, Hokkaido|Ishikari District, Hokkaido]] under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu]].{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: [[:en:1716|1716]] ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – [[:en:1770|1770]] ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
: The son of Shimazaki Hanbē from [[:en:Tsuruga Port|Tsuruga Port]]. He worked for the Matsumae branch of the Omi merchant Manya Kiemon before being adopted as Denbē's son-in-law.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: [[:en:1738|1738]] ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa era]], he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By [[:en:1775|1775]] ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in [[:en:1778|1778]] ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the [[:en:Mashike District, Hokkaido|Mashike District, Hokkaido]] trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and [[:en:Machi-bugyō|Machi bugyō]] assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In [[:en:1789|1789]] ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the [[:en:Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion]], the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In [[:en:1790|1790]] ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in [[:en:Sakhalin|Sakhalin]], and by [[:en:1798|1798]] ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in [[:en:1796|1796]] ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in [[:en:1799|1799]] ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In [[:en:1915|1915]] ([[:en:1915|Taisho 3]]), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

==Overview==
'''Murayama Denbē''' (村山 伝兵衛) was a merchant who expanded from [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]] to [[:en:Ezo|Ezo]] during the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku era]]. The name "Denbē" was passed down through successive heads of the Murayama family.

The Murayama family's business was known as '''Abe-ya''' (安部屋 or 阿部屋), and their shop emblem was '''Marujūgo''' (○に十五), symbolizing the first Denbē's ambition to own 15 ships.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

== Successive Heads of the Murayama Family ==

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in [[:en:Hakui District, Ishikawa|Hakui District]], Noto Province, he moved to [[:en:Matsumae Domain|Matsumae Domain]] in his late teens or early twenties. He initially stayed at the home of a Matsumae retainer, Kudō Hachirōemon, while starting his business.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}} Some records indicate he may have worked as a servant for Harue Jinbē, who had already established Abe-ya in Matsumae.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in [[:en:Sōya District, Hokkaido|Sōya District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Tomamae District, Hokkaido|Tomamae District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Rumoi District, Hokkaido|Rumoi District, Hokkaido]], and [[:en:Ishikari District, Hokkaido|Ishikari District, Hokkaido]] under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu]].{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: [[:en:1716|1716]] ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – [[:en:1770|1770]] ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
: The son of Shimazaki Hanbē from [[:en:Tsuruga Port|Tsuruga Port]]. He worked for the Matsumae branch of the Omi merchant Manya Kiemon before being adopted as Denbē's son-in-law.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: [[:en:1738|1738]] ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa era]], he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By [[:en:1775|1775]] ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in [[:en:1778|1778]] ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the [[:en:Mashike District, Hokkaido|Mashike District, Hokkaido]] trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and [[:en:Machi-bugyō|Machi bugyō]] assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In [[:en:1789|1789]] ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the [[:en:Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion]], the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In [[:en:1790|1790]] ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in [[:en:Sakhalin|Sakhalin]], and by [[:en:1798|1798]] ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in [[:en:1796|1796]] ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in [[:en:1799|1799]] ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In [[:en:1915|1915]] ([[:en:1915|Taisho 3]]), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at [[:en:Hokkaidō Shrine|Hokkaido Shrine]]'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in [[:en:Minami-ku, Sapporo|Minami-ku, Sapporo]] was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on [[:en:Japan National Route 230|National Route 230 (Japan)]].{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

[[Matsumae Domain]] trader and agent {{ill|Murayama Denbee|ja|村山伝兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Denbee|qq=}} (1738–1813) is said to have founded the small shrine of {{nihongo|Sōya Daijingū|宗谷大神宮}} In [[:en:Tenmei|Tenmei]] 5 (1785), enshrining [[Amaterasu]] as guardian of the north gate.<ref name="Jinjacho">{{cite web |url=https://hokkaidojinjacho.jp/北門神社/ |script-title=ja:北門神社 |trans-title=Hokumon Jinja |language=Japanese |publisher=Hokkaidō Jinja-chō |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.city.wakkanai.hokkaido.jp/gaiyo/ayumi.html |script-title=ja:稚内のあゆみ（歴史）|trans-title=History of Wakkanai |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Wakkanai, Hokkaido|Wakkanai City]] |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref> In 1896 the shrine was transferred to its current location and renamed [[Hokumon Shrine]], with [[Takemikazuchi]] and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] enshrined alongside Amaterasu.<ref name="Jinjacho"/>

[[Matsumae Domain]] trader and agent {{ill|Murayama Denbee|ja|村山伝兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Denbee|qq=}} (1738–1813) is said to have founded the small shrine of {{nihongo|Sōya Daijingū|宗谷大神宮}} In [[:en:Tenmei|Tenmei]] 5 (1785), enshrining [[Amaterasu]] as guardian of the north gate.<ref name="Jinjacho">{{cite web |url=https://hokkaidojinjacho.jp/北門神社/ |script-title=ja:北門神社 |trans-title=Hokumon Jinja |language=Japanese |publisher=Hokkaidō Jinja-chō |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.city.wakkanai.hokkaido.jp/gaiyo/ayumi.html |script-title=ja:稚内のあゆみ（歴史）|trans-title=History of Wakkanai |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Wakkanai, Hokkaido|Wakkanai City]] |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref> In 1896 the shrine was transferred to its current location and renamed [[Hokumon Shrine]], with [[Takemikazuchi]] and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] enshrined alongside Amaterasu.<ref name="Jinjacho"/>

==Overview==
'''Murayama Denbē''' (村山 伝兵衛) was a merchant who expanded from [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]] to [[:en:Ezo|Ezo]] during the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku era]]. The name "Denbē" was passed down through successive heads of the Murayama family.

The Murayama family's business was known as '''Abe-ya''' (安部屋 or 阿部屋), and their shop emblem was '''Marujūgo''' (○に十五), symbolizing the first Denbē's ambition to own 15 ships.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

== Successive Heads of the Murayama Family ==

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in [[:en:Hakui District, Ishikawa|Hakui District]], Noto Province, he moved to [[:en:Matsumae Domain|Matsumae Domain]] in his late teens or early twenties. He initially stayed at the home of a Matsumae retainer, Kudō Hachirōemon, while starting his business.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}} Some records indicate he may have worked as a servant for Harue Jinbē, who had already established Abe-ya in Matsumae.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in [[:en:Sōya District, Hokkaido|Sōya District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Tomamae District, Hokkaido|Tomamae District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Rumoi District, Hokkaido|Rumoi District, Hokkaido]], and [[:en:Ishikari District, Hokkaido|Ishikari District, Hokkaido]] under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu]].{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: [[:en:1716|1716]] ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – [[:en:1770|1770]] ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
: The son of Shimazaki Hanbē from [[:en:Tsuruga Port|Tsuruga Port]]. He worked for the Matsumae branch of the Omi merchant Manya Kiemon before being adopted as Denbē's son-in-law.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: [[:en:1738|1738]] ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa era]], he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By [[:en:1775|1775]] ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in [[:en:1778|1778]] ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the [[:en:Mashike District, Hokkaido|Mashike District, Hokkaido]] trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and [[:en:Machi-bugyō|Machi bugyō]] assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In [[:en:1789|1789]] ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the [[:en:Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion]], the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In [[:en:1790|1790]] ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in [[:en:Sakhalin|Sakhalin]], and by [[:en:1798|1798]] ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in [[:en:1796|1796]] ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in [[:en:1799|1799]] ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In [[:en:1915|1915]] ([[:en:1915|Taisho 3]]), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Murayama Family Genealogy ==

{{familytree| | | | | |,|-|^|-|.| |}}

{{familytree/end}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at [[:en:Hokkaidō Shrine|Hokkaido Shrine]]'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in [[:en:Minami-ku, Sapporo|Minami-ku, Sapporo]] was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on [[:en:Japan National Route 230|National Route 230 (Japan)]].{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at [[:en:Hokkaidō Shrine|Hokkaido Shrine]]'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in [[:en:Minami-ku, Sapporo|Minami-ku, Sapporo]] was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on [[:en:Japan National Route 230|National Route 230 (Japan)]].{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

[[Matsumae Domain]] trader and agent {{ill|Murayama Denbee|ja|村山伝兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Denbee|qq=}} (1738–1813) is said to have founded the small shrine of {{nihongo|Sōya Daijingū|宗谷大神宮}} In [[:en:Tenmei|Tenmei]] 5 (1785), enshrining [[Amaterasu]] as guardian of the north gate.<ref name="Jinjacho">{{cite web |url=https://hokkaidojinjacho.jp/北門神社/ |script-title=ja:北門神社 |trans-title=Hokumon Jinja |language=Japanese |publisher=Hokkaidō Jinja-chō |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.city.wakkanai.hokkaido.jp/gaiyo/ayumi.html |script-title=ja:稚内のあゆみ（歴史）|trans-title=History of Wakkanai |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Wakkanai, Hokkaido|Wakkanai City]] |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref> In 1896 the shrine was transferred to its current location and renamed [[Hokumon Shrine]], with [[Takemikazuchi]] and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] enshrined alongside Amaterasu.<ref name="Jinjacho"/>

==Overview==
'''Murayama Denbē''' (村山 伝兵衛) was a merchant who expanded from [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]] to [[:en:Ezo|Ezo]] during the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku era]]. The name "Denbē" was passed down through successive heads of the Murayama family.

The Murayama family's business was known as '''Abe-ya''' (安部屋 or 阿部屋), and their shop emblem was '''Marujūgo''' (○に十五), symbolizing the first Denbē's ambition to own 15 ships.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

== Successive Heads of the Murayama Family ==

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in [[:en:Hakui District, Ishikawa|Hakui District]], Noto Province, he moved to [[:en:Matsumae Domain|Matsumae Domain]] in his late teens or early twenties. He initially stayed at the home of a Matsumae retainer, Kudō Hachirōemon, while starting his business.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}} Some records indicate he may have worked as a servant for Harue Jinbē, who had already established Abe-ya in Matsumae.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in [[:en:Sōya District, Hokkaido|Sōya District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Tomamae District, Hokkaido|Tomamae District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Rumoi District, Hokkaido|Rumoi District, Hokkaido]], and [[:en:Ishikari District, Hokkaido|Ishikari District, Hokkaido]] under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu]].{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: [[:en:1716|1716]] ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – [[:en:1770|1770]] ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
: The son of Shimazaki Hanbē from [[:en:Tsuruga Port|Tsuruga Port]]. He worked for the Matsumae branch of the Omi merchant Manya Kiemon before being adopted as Denbē's son-in-law.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: [[:en:1738|1738]] ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa era]], he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By [[:en:1775|1775]] ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in [[:en:1778|1778]] ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the [[:en:Mashike District, Hokkaido|Mashike District, Hokkaido]] trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and [[:en:Machi-bugyō|Machi bugyō]] assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In [[:en:1789|1789]] ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the [[:en:Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion]], the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In [[:en:1790|1790]] ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in [[:en:Sakhalin|Sakhalin]], and by [[:en:1798|1798]] ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in [[:en:1796|1796]] ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in [[:en:1799|1799]] ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In [[:en:1915|1915]] ([[:en:1915|Taisho 3]]), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at [[:en:Hokkaidō Shrine|Hokkaido Shrine]]'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in [[:en:Minami-ku, Sapporo|Minami-ku, Sapporo]] was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on [[:en:Japan National Route 230|National Route 230 (Japan)]].{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and [[:en:Machi-bugyō|Machi bugyō]] assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In [[:en:1789|1789]] ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the [[:en:Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion]], the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In [[:en:1790|1790]] ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in [[:en:Sakhalin|Sakhalin]], and by [[:en:1798|1798]] ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in [[:en:1796|1796]] ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in [[:en:1799|1799]] ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In [[:en:1915|1915]] ([[:en:1915|Taisho 3]]), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Murayama Family Genealogy ==

{{familytree| | | | | DE1 | | SHI | |DE1='''First Generation: Denbē'''|SHI=Shimazaki Hanbē}}
{{familytree| | | | | |!| | | |!| |}}
{{familytree| | | | | DAU |y| RIH |DAU=Daughter|RIH='''Second Generation: Rihei'''}}
{{familytree| | | | | |,|-|^|-|.| |}}
{{familytree| NIS | | BOY | | DE3 |NIS=Nishioka Jizaimon|BOY=Second Son|DE3='''Third Generation: Denbē'''}}
{{familytree/end}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at [[:en:Hokkaidō Shrine|Hokkaido Shrine]]'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in [[:en:Minami-ku, Sapporo|Minami-ku, Sapporo]] was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on [[:en:Japan National Route 230|National Route 230 (Japan)]].{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

[[Matsumae Domain]] trader and agent {{ill|Murayama Denbee|ja|村山伝兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Denbee|qq=}} (1738–1813) is said to have founded the small shrine of {{nihongo|Sōya Daijingū|宗谷大神宮}} In [[:en:Tenmei|Tenmei]] 5 (1785), enshrining [[Amaterasu]] as guardian of the north gate.<ref name="Jinjacho">{{cite web |url=https://hokkaidojinjacho.jp/北門神社/ |script-title=ja:北門神社 |trans-title=Hokumon Jinja |language=Japanese |publisher=Hokkaidō Jinja-chō |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.city.wakkanai.hokkaido.jp/gaiyo/ayumi.html |script-title=ja:稚内のあゆみ（歴史）|trans-title=History of Wakkanai |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Wakkanai, Hokkaido|Wakkanai City]] |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref> In 1896 the shrine was transferred to its current location and renamed [[Hokumon Shrine]], with [[Takemikazuchi]] and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] enshrined alongside Amaterasu.<ref name="Jinjacho"/>

==Overview==
'''Murayama Denbē''' (村山 伝兵衛) was a merchant who expanded from [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]] to [[:en:Ezo|Ezo]] during the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku era]]. The name "Denbē" was passed down through successive heads of the Murayama family.

The Murayama family's business was known as '''Abe-ya''' (安部屋 or 阿部屋), and their shop emblem was '''Marujūgo''' (○に十五), symbolizing the first Denbē's ambition to own 15 ships.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

== Successive Heads of the Murayama Family ==

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in [[:en:Hakui District, Ishikawa|Hakui District]], Noto Province, he moved to [[:en:Matsumae Domain|Matsumae Domain]] in his late teens or early twenties. He initially stayed at the home of a Matsumae retainer, Kudō Hachirōemon, while starting his business.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}} Some records indicate he may have worked as a servant for Harue Jinbē, who had already established Abe-ya in Matsumae.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in [[:en:Sōya District, Hokkaido|Sōya District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Tomamae District, Hokkaido|Tomamae District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Rumoi District, Hokkaido|Rumoi District, Hokkaido]], and [[:en:Ishikari District, Hokkaido|Ishikari District, Hokkaido]] under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu]].{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: [[:en:1716|1716]] ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – [[:en:1770|1770]] ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
: The son of Shimazaki Hanbē from [[:en:Tsuruga Port|Tsuruga Port]]. He worked for the Matsumae branch of the Omi merchant Manya Kiemon before being adopted as Denbē's son-in-law.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: [[:en:1738|1738]] ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa era]], he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By [[:en:1775|1775]] ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in [[:en:1778|1778]] ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the [[:en:Mashike District, Hokkaido|Mashike District, Hokkaido]] trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and [[:en:Machi-bugyō|Machi bugyō]] assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In [[:en:1789|1789]] ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the [[:en:Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion]], the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In [[:en:1790|1790]] ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in [[:en:Sakhalin|Sakhalin]], and by [[:en:1798|1798]] ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in [[:en:1796|1796]] ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in [[:en:1799|1799]] ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In [[:en:1915|1915]] ([[:en:1915|Taisho 3]]), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at [[:en:Hokkaidō Shrine|Hokkaido Shrine]]'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in [[:en:Minami-ku, Sapporo|Minami-ku, Sapporo]] was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on [[:en:Japan National Route 230|National Route 230 (Japan)]].{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in [[:en:Hakui District, Ishikawa|Hakui District]], Noto Province, he moved to [[:en:Matsumae Domain|Matsumae Domain]] in his late teens or early twenties. : To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in [[:en:Sōya District, Hokkaido|Sōya District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Tomamae District, Hokkaido|Tomamae District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Rumoi District, Hokkaido|Rumoi District, Hokkaido]], and [[:en:Ishikari District, Hokkaido|Ishikari District, Hokkaido]] under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu]].{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: [[:en:1716|1716]] ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – [[:en:1770|1770]] ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
: The son of Shimazaki Hanbē from [[:en:Tsuruga Port|Tsuruga Port]]. He worked for the Matsumae branch of the Omi merchant Manya Kiemon before being adopted as Denbē's son-in-law.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: [[:en:1738|1738]] ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa era]], he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By [[:en:1775|1775]] ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in [[:en:1778|1778]] ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the [[:en:Mashike District, Hokkaido|Mashike District, Hokkaido]] trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and [[:en:Machi-bugyō|Machi bugyō]] assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In [[:en:1789|1789]] ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the [[:en:Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion]], the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In [[:en:1790|1790]] ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in [[:en:Sakhalin|Sakhalin]], and by [[:en:1798|1798]] ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in [[:en:1796|1796]] ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in [[:en:1799|1799]] ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In [[:en:1915|1915]] ([[:en:1915|Taisho 3]]), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at [[:en:Hokkaidō Shrine|Hokkaido Shrine]]'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in [[:en:Minami-ku, Sapporo|Minami-ku, Sapporo]] was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on [[:en:Japan National Route 230|National Route 230 (Japan)]].{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

[[Matsumae Domain]] trader and agent {{ill|Murayama Denbee|ja|村山伝兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Denbee|qq=}} (1738–1813) is said to have founded the small shrine of {{nihongo|Sōya Daijingū|宗谷大神宮}} In [[:en:Tenmei|Tenmei]] 5 (1785), enshrining [[Amaterasu]] as guardian of the north gate.<ref name="Jinjacho">{{cite web |url=https://hokkaidojinjacho.jp/北門神社/ |script-title=ja:北門神社 |trans-title=Hokumon Jinja |language=Japanese |publisher=Hokkaidō Jinja-chō |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.city.wakkanai.hokkaido.jp/gaiyo/ayumi.html |script-title=ja:稚内のあゆみ（歴史）|trans-title=History of Wakkanai |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Wakkanai, Hokkaido|Wakkanai City]] |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref> In 1896 the shrine was transferred to its current location and renamed [[Hokumon Shrine]], with [[Takemikazuchi]] and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] enshrined alongside Amaterasu.<ref name="Jinjacho"/>

==Overview==
'''Murayama Denbē''' (村山 伝兵衛) was a merchant who expanded from [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]] to [[:en:Ezo|Ezo]] during the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku era]]. The name "Denbē" was passed down through successive heads of the Murayama family.

The Murayama family's business was known as '''Abe-ya''' (安部屋 or 阿部屋), and their shop emblem was '''Marujūgo''' (○に十五), symbolizing the first Denbē's ambition to own 15 ships.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

== Successive Heads of the Murayama Family ==

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in [[:en:Hakui District, Ishikawa|Hakui District]], Noto Province, he moved to [[:en:Matsumae Domain|Matsumae Domain]] in his late teens or early twenties. He initially stayed at the home of a Matsumae retainer, Kudō Hachirōemon, while starting his business.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}} Some records indicate he may have worked as a servant for Harue Jinbē, who had already established Abe-ya in Matsumae.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in [[:en:Sōya District, Hokkaido|Sōya District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Tomamae District, Hokkaido|Tomamae District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Rumoi District, Hokkaido|Rumoi District, Hokkaido]], and [[:en:Ishikari District, Hokkaido|Ishikari District, Hokkaido]] under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu]].{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: [[:en:1716|1716]] ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – [[:en:1770|1770]] ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
: The son of Shimazaki Hanbē from [[:en:Tsuruga Port|Tsuruga Port]]. He worked for the Matsumae branch of the Omi merchant Manya Kiemon before being adopted as Denbē's son-in-law.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: [[:en:1738|1738]] ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa era]], he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By [[:en:1775|1775]] ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in [[:en:1778|1778]] ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the [[:en:Mashike District, Hokkaido|Mashike District, Hokkaido]] trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and [[:en:Machi-bugyō|Machi bugyō]] assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In [[:en:1789|1789]] ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the [[:en:Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion]], the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In [[:en:1790|1790]] ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in [[:en:Sakhalin|Sakhalin]], and by [[:en:1798|1798]] ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in [[:en:1796|1796]] ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in [[:en:1799|1799]] ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In [[:en:1915|1915]] ([[:en:1915|Taisho 3]]), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}
