[[Matsumae Domain]] trader and agent {{ill|Murayama Denbee|ja|村山伝兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Denbee|qq=}} (1738–1813) is said to have founded the small shrine of {{nihongo|Sōya Daijingū|宗谷大神宮}} In [[:en:Tenmei|Tenmei]] 5 (1785), enshrining [[Amaterasu]] as guardian of the north gate.<ref name="Jinjacho">{{cite web |url=https://hokkaidojinjacho.jp/北門神社/ |script-title=ja:北門神社 |trans-title=Hokumon Jinja |language=Japanese |publisher=Hokkaidō Jinja-chō |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.city.wakkanai.hokkaido.jp/gaiyo/ayumi.html |script-title=ja:稚内のあゆみ（歴史）|trans-title=History of Wakkanai |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Wakkanai, Hokkaido|Wakkanai City]] |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref> In 1896 the shrine was transferred to its current location and renamed [[Hokumon Shrine]], with [[Takemikazuchi]] and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] enshrined alongside Amaterasu.<ref name="Jinjacho"/>

==Overview==
'''Murayama Denbē''' (村山 伝兵衛) was a merchant who expanded from [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]] to [[:en:Ezo|Ezo]] during the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku era]]. The name "Denbē" was passed down through successive heads of the Murayama family.

The Murayama family's business was known as '''Abe-ya''' (安部屋 or 阿部屋), and their shop emblem was '''Marujūgo''' (○に十五), symbolizing the first Denbē's ambition to own 15 ships.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

== Successive Heads of the Murayama Family ==

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in [[:en:Hakui District, Ishikawa|Hakui District]], Noto Province, he moved to [[:en:Matsumae Domain|Matsumae Domain]] in his late teens or early twenties. He initially stayed at the home of a Matsumae retainer, Kudō Hachirōemon, while starting his business.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}} Some records indicate he may have worked as a servant for Harue Jinbē, who had already established Abe-ya in Matsumae.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in [[:en:Sōya District, Hokkaido|Sōya District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Tomamae District, Hokkaido|Tomamae District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Rumoi District, Hokkaido|Rumoi District, Hokkaido]], and [[:en:Ishikari District, Hokkaido|Ishikari District, Hokkaido]] under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu]].{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: [[:en:1716|1716]] ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – [[:en:1770|1770]] ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
: The son of Shimazaki Hanbē from [[:en:Tsuruga Port|Tsuruga Port]]. He worked for the Matsumae branch of the Omi merchant Manya Kiemon before being adopted as Denbē's son-in-law.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: [[:en:1738|1738]] ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa era]], he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By [[:en:1775|1775]] ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in [[:en:1778|1778]] ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the [[:en:Mashike District, Hokkaido|Mashike District, Hokkaido]] trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and [[:en:Machi-bugyō|Machi bugyō]] assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In [[:en:1789|1789]] ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the [[:en:Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion]], the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In [[:en:1790|1790]] ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in [[:en:Sakhalin|Sakhalin]], and by [[:en:1798|1798]] ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in [[:en:1796|1796]] ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in [[:en:1799|1799]] ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In [[:en:1915|1915]] ([[:en:1915|Taisho 3]]), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at [[:en:Hokkaidō Shrine|Hokkaido Shrine]]'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in [[:en:Minami-ku, Sapporo|Minami-ku, Sapporo]] was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on [[:en:Japan National Route 230|National Route 230 (Japan)]].{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at [[:en:Hokkaidō Shrine|Hokkaido Shrine]]'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. [[Matsumae Domain]] trader and agent {{ill|Murayama Denbee|ja|村山伝兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Denbee|qq=}} (1738–1813) is said to have founded the small shrine of {{nihongo|Sōya Daijingū|宗谷大神宮}} In [[:en:Tenmei|Tenmei]] 5 (1785), enshrining [[Amaterasu]] as guardian of the north gate.<ref name="Jinjacho">{{cite web |url=https://hokkaidojinjacho.jp/北門神社/ |script-title=ja:北門神社 |trans-title=Hokumon Jinja |language=Japanese |publisher=Hokkaidō Jinja-chō |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.city.wakkanai.hokkaido.jp/gaiyo/ayumi.html |script-title=ja:稚内のあゆみ（歴史）|trans-title=History of Wakkanai |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Wakkanai, Hokkaido|Wakkanai City]] |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref> In 1896 the shrine was transferred to its current location and renamed [[Hokumon Shrine]], with [[Takemikazuchi]] and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] enshrined alongside Amaterasu.<ref name="Jinjacho"/>

==Overview==
'''Murayama Denbē''' (村山 伝兵衛) was a merchant who expanded from [[:en:Noto Province|Noto Province]] to [[:en:Ezo|Ezo]] during the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku era]]. The name "Denbē" was passed down through successive heads of the Murayama family.

The Murayama family's business was known as '''Abe-ya''' (安部屋 or 阿部屋), and their shop emblem was '''Marujūgo''' (○に十五), symbolizing the first Denbē's ambition to own 15 ships.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

== Successive Heads of the Murayama Family ==

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in [[:en:Hakui District, Ishikawa|Hakui District]], Noto Province, he moved to [[:en:Matsumae Domain|Matsumae Domain]] in his late teens or early twenties. He initially stayed at the home of a Matsumae retainer, Kudō Hachirōemon, while starting his business.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}} Some records indicate he may have worked as a servant for Harue Jinbē, who had already established Abe-ya in Matsumae.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in [[:en:Sōya District, Hokkaido|Sōya District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Tomamae District, Hokkaido|Tomamae District, Hokkaido]], [[:en:Rumoi District, Hokkaido|Rumoi District, Hokkaido]], and [[:en:Ishikari District, Hokkaido|Ishikari District, Hokkaido]] under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the [[:en:Ainu people|Ainu]].{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: [[:en:1716|1716]] ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – [[:en:1770|1770]] ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
: The son of Shimazaki Hanbē from [[:en:Tsuruga Port|Tsuruga Port]]. He worked for the Matsumae branch of the Omi merchant Manya Kiemon before being adopted as Denbē's son-in-law.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: [[:en:1738|1738]] ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the [[:en:Meiwa|Meiwa era]], he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By [[:en:1775|1775]] ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in [[:en:1778|1778]] ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the [[:en:Mashike District, Hokkaido|Mashike District, Hokkaido]] trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and [[:en:Machi-bugyō|Machi bugyō]] assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In [[:en:1789|1789]] ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the [[:en:Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion]], the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In [[:en:1790|1790]] ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in [[:en:Sakhalin|Sakhalin]], and by [[:en:1798|1798]] ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in [[:en:1796|1796]] ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in [[:en:1799|1799]] ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In [[:en:1915|1915]] ([[:en:1915|Taisho 3]]), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Murayama Family Genealogy ==

{{familytree| | | | | |,|-|^|-|.| |}}

{{familytree/end}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at [[:en:Hokkaidō Shrine|Hokkaido Shrine]]'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in [[:en:Minami-ku, Sapporo|Minami-ku, Sapporo]] was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on [[:en:Japan National Route 230|National Route 230 (Japan)]].{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

== Successive Heads of the Murayama Family ==

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in {{ill|Hakui District, Ishikawa|en|Hakui District, Ishikawa|WD=Q1130953|lt=Hakui District}}, Noto Province, he moved to {{ill|Matsumae Domain|en|Matsumae Domain|WD=Q18669712|lt=Matsumae Domain}} in his late teens or early twenties. He initially stayed at the home of a Matsumae retainer, Kudō Hachirōemon, while starting his business.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}} Some records indicate he may have worked as a servant for Harue Jinbē, who had already established Abe-ya in Matsumae.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in {{ill|Sōya District, Hokkaido|en|Sōya District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1185957|lt=Sōya District, Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Tomamae District, Hokkaido|en|Tomamae District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134113|lt=Tomamae District, Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Rumoi District, Hokkaido|en|Rumoi District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134204|lt=Rumoi District, Hokkaido}}, and {{ill|Ishikari District, Hokkaido|en|Ishikari District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1185986|lt=Ishikari District, Hokkaido}} under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|WD=Q101828|lt=Ainu}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: {{ill|1716|en|1716|WD=Q6892|lt=1716}} ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|1770|en|1770|WD=Q7683|lt=1770}} ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})

: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: {{ill|1738|en|1738|WD=Q6996|lt=1738}} ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era}}, he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By {{ill|1775|en|1775|WD=Q7700|lt=1775}} ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in {{ill|1778|en|1778|WD=Q7713|lt=1778}} ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the {{ill|Mashike District, Hokkaido|en|Mashike District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134336|lt=Mashike District, Hokkaido}} trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and {{ill|Machi-bugyō|en|Machi-bugyō|WD=Q908892|lt=Machi bugyō}} assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In {{ill|1789|en|1789|WD=Q7772|lt=1789}} ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the {{ill|Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|en|Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|WD=Q3454489|lt=Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion}}, the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In {{ill|1790|en|1790|WD=Q7775|lt=1790}} ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in {{ill|Sakhalin|en|Sakhalin|WD=Q7792|lt=Sakhalin}}, and by {{ill|1798|en|1798|WD=Q7823|lt=1798}} ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in {{ill|1796|en|1796|WD=Q7818|lt=1796}} ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in {{ill|1799|en|1799|WD=Q7827|lt=1799}} ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In {{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=1915}} ({{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=Taisho 3}}), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at {{ill|Hokkaidō Shrine|en|Hokkaidō Shrine|WD=Q710826|lt=Hokkaido Shrine}}'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in {{ill|Minami-ku, Sapporo|en|Minami-ku, Sapporo|WD=Q1134027|lt=Minami-ku, Sapporo}} was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on {{ill|Japan National Route 230|en|Japan National Route 230|WD=Q11421425|lt=National Route 230 (Japan)}}.{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

[[Matsumae Domain]] trader and agent {{ill|Murayama Denbee|ja|村山伝兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Denbee|qq=}} (1738–1813) is said to have founded the small shrine of {{nihongo|Sōya Daijingū|宗谷大神宮}} In {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei}} 5 (1785), enshrining [[Amaterasu]] as guardian of the north gate.<ref name="Jinjacho">{{cite web |url=https://hokkaidojinjacho.jp/北門神社/ |script-title=ja:北門神社 |trans-title=Hokumon Jinja |language=Japanese |publisher=Hokkaidō Jinja-chō |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.city.wakkanai.hokkaido.jp/gaiyo/ayumi.html |script-title=ja:稚内のあゆみ（歴史）|trans-title=History of Wakkanai |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Wakkanai, Hokkaido|Wakkanai City]] |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref> In 1896 the shrine was transferred to its current location and renamed [[Hokumon Shrine]], with [[Takemikazuchi]] and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] enshrined alongside Amaterasu.<ref name="Jinjacho"/>

==Overview==
'''Murayama Denbē''' (村山 伝兵衛) was a merchant who expanded from {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province}} to {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo}} during the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku era}}. The name "Denbē" was passed down through successive heads of the Murayama family.

The Murayama family's business was known as '''Abe-ya''' (安部屋 or 阿部屋), and their shop emblem was '''Marujūgo''' (○に十五), symbolizing the first Denbē's ambition to own 15 ships.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

== Successive Heads of the Murayama Family ==

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in {{ill|Hakui District, Ishikawa|en|Hakui District, Ishikawa|WD=Q1130953|lt=Hakui District}}, Noto Province, he moved to {{ill|Matsumae Domain|en|Matsumae Domain|WD=Q18669712|lt=Matsumae Domain}} in his late teens or early twenties. He initially stayed at the home of a Matsumae retainer, Kudō Hachirōemon, while starting his business.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}} Some records indicate he may have worked as a servant for Harue Jinbē, who had already established Abe-ya in Matsumae.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in {{ill|Sōya District, Hokkaido|en|Sōya District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1185957|lt=Sōya District, Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Tomamae District, Hokkaido|en|Tomamae District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134113|lt=Tomamae District, Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Rumoi District, Hokkaido|en|Rumoi District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134204|lt=Rumoi District, Hokkaido}}, and {{ill|Ishikari District, Hokkaido|en|Ishikari District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1185986|lt=Ishikari District, Hokkaido}} under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|WD=Q101828|lt=Ainu}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: {{ill|1716|en|1716|WD=Q6892|lt=1716}} ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|1770|en|1770|WD=Q7683|lt=1770}} ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
: The son of Shimazaki Hanbē from {{ill|Tsuruga Port|en|Tsuruga Port|WD=Q7850265|lt=Tsuruga Port}}. He worked for the Matsumae branch of the Omi merchant Manya Kiemon before being adopted as Denbē's son-in-law.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: {{ill|1738|en|1738|WD=Q6996|lt=1738}} ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era}}, he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By {{ill|1775|en|1775|WD=Q7700|lt=1775}} ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in {{ill|1778|en|1778|WD=Q7713|lt=1778}} ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the {{ill|Mashike District, Hokkaido|en|Mashike District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134336|lt=Mashike District, Hokkaido}} trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and {{ill|Machi-bugyō|en|Machi-bugyō|WD=Q908892|lt=Machi bugyō}} assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In {{ill|1789|en|1789|WD=Q7772|lt=1789}} ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the {{ill|Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|en|Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|WD=Q3454489|lt=Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion}}, the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In {{ill|1790|en|1790|WD=Q7775|lt=1790}} ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in {{ill|Sakhalin|en|Sakhalin|WD=Q7792|lt=Sakhalin}}, and by {{ill|1798|en|1798|WD=Q7823|lt=1798}} ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in {{ill|1796|en|1796|WD=Q7818|lt=1796}} ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in {{ill|1799|en|1799|WD=Q7827|lt=1799}} ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In {{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=1915}} ({{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=Taisho 3}}), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at {{ill|Hokkaidō Shrine|en|Hokkaidō Shrine|WD=Q710826|lt=Hokkaido Shrine}}'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in {{ill|Minami-ku, Sapporo|en|Minami-ku, Sapporo|WD=Q1134027|lt=Minami-ku, Sapporo}} was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on {{ill|Japan National Route 230|en|Japan National Route 230|WD=Q11421425|lt=National Route 230 (Japan)}}.{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

[[Matsumae Domain]] trader and agent {{ill|Murayama Denbee|ja|村山伝兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Denbee|qq=}} (1738–1813) is said to have founded the small shrine of {{nihongo|Sōya Daijingū|宗谷大神宮}} In {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei}} 5 (1785), enshrining [[Amaterasu]] as guardian of the north gate.<ref name="Jinjacho">{{cite web |url=https://hokkaidojinjacho.jp/北門神社/ |script-title=ja:北門神社 |trans-title=Hokumon Jinja |language=Japanese |publisher=Hokkaidō Jinja-chō |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.city.wakkanai.hokkaido.jp/gaiyo/ayumi.html |script-title=ja:稚内のあゆみ（歴史）|trans-title=History of Wakkanai |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Wakkanai, Hokkaido|Wakkanai City]] |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref> In 1896 the shrine was transferred to its current location and renamed [[Hokumon Shrine]], with [[Takemikazuchi]] and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] enshrined alongside Amaterasu.<ref name="Jinjacho"/>

==Overview==
'''Murayama Denbē''' (村山 伝兵衛) was a merchant who expanded from {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province}} to {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo}} during the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku era}}. The name "Denbē" was passed down through successive heads of the Murayama family.

The Murayama family's business was known as '''Abe-ya''' (安部屋 or 阿部屋), and their shop emblem was '''Marujūgo''' (○に十五), symbolizing the first Denbē's ambition to own 15 ships.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

== Successive Heads of the Murayama Family ==

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in {{ill|Hakui District, Ishikawa|en|Hakui District, Ishikawa|WD=Q1130953|lt=Hakui District}}, Noto Province, he moved to {{ill|Matsumae Domain|en|Matsumae Domain|WD=Q18669712|lt=Matsumae Domain}} in his late teens or early twenties. He initially stayed at the home of a Matsumae retainer, Kudō Hachirōemon, while starting his business.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}} Some records indicate he may have worked as a servant for Harue Jinbē, who had already established Abe-ya in Matsumae.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in {{ill|Sōya District, Hokkaido|en|Sōya District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1185957|lt=Sōya District, Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Tomamae District, Hokkaido|en|Tomamae District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134113|lt=Tomamae District, Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Rumoi District, Hokkaido|en|Rumoi District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134204|lt=Rumoi District, Hokkaido}}, and {{ill|Ishikari District, Hokkaido|en|Ishikari District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1185986|lt=Ishikari District, Hokkaido}} under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|WD=Q101828|lt=Ainu}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: {{ill|1716|en|1716|WD=Q6892|lt=1716}} ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|1770|en|1770|WD=Q7683|lt=1770}} ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
: The son of Shimazaki Hanbē from {{ill|Tsuruga Port|en|Tsuruga Port|WD=Q7850265|lt=Tsuruga Port}}. He worked for the Matsumae branch of the Omi merchant Manya Kiemon before being adopted as Denbē's son-in-law.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: {{ill|1738|en|1738|WD=Q6996|lt=1738}} ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era}}, he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By {{ill|1775|en|1775|WD=Q7700|lt=1775}} ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in {{ill|1778|en|1778|WD=Q7713|lt=1778}} ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the {{ill|Mashike District, Hokkaido|en|Mashike District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134336|lt=Mashike District, Hokkaido}} trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and {{ill|Machi-bugyō|en|Machi-bugyō|WD=Q908892|lt=Machi bugyō}} assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In {{ill|1789|en|1789|WD=Q7772|lt=1789}} ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the {{ill|Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|en|Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|WD=Q3454489|lt=Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion}}, the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In {{ill|1790|en|1790|WD=Q7775|lt=1790}} ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in {{ill|Sakhalin|en|Sakhalin|WD=Q7792|lt=Sakhalin}}, and by {{ill|1798|en|1798|WD=Q7823|lt=1798}} ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in {{ill|1796|en|1796|WD=Q7818|lt=1796}} ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in {{ill|1799|en|1799|WD=Q7827|lt=1799}} ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In {{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=1915}} ({{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=Taisho 3}}), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at {{ill|Hokkaidō Shrine|en|Hokkaidō Shrine|WD=Q710826|lt=Hokkaido Shrine}}'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in {{ill|Minami-ku, Sapporo|en|Minami-ku, Sapporo|WD=Q1134027|lt=Minami-ku, Sapporo}} was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on {{ill|Japan National Route 230|en|Japan National Route 230|WD=Q11421425|lt=National Route 230 (Japan)}}.{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:村山伝兵衛]]






[[Matsumae Domain]] trader and agent {{ill|Murayama Denbee|ja|村山伝兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Denbee|qq=}} (1738–1813) is said to have founded the small shrine of {{nihongo|Sōya Daijingū|宗谷大神宮}} In {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei}} 5 (1785), enshrining [[Amaterasu]] as guardian of the north gate.<ref name="Jinjacho">{{cite web |url=https://hokkaidojinjacho.jp/北門神社/ |script-title=ja:北門神社 |trans-title=Hokumon Jinja |language=Japanese |publisher=Hokkaidō Jinja-chō |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.city.wakkanai.hokkaido.jp/gaiyo/ayumi.html |script-title=ja:稚内のあゆみ（歴史）|trans-title=History of Wakkanai |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Wakkanai, Hokkaido|Wakkanai City]] |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref> In 1896 the shrine was transferred to its current location and renamed [[Hokumon Shrine]], with [[Takemikazuchi]] and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] enshrined alongside Amaterasu.<ref name="Jinjacho"/>

==Overview==
'''Murayama Denbē''' (村山 伝兵衛) was a merchant who expanded from {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province}} to {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo}} during the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku era}}. The name "Denbē" was passed down through successive heads of the Murayama family.

The Murayama family's business was known as '''Abe-ya''' (安部屋 or 阿部屋), and their shop emblem was '''Marujūgo''' (○に十五), symbolizing the first Denbē's ambition to own 15 ships.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

== Successive Heads of the Murayama Family ==

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in {{ill|Hakui District, Ishikawa|en|Hakui District, Ishikawa|WD=Q1130953|lt=Hakui District}}, Noto Province, he moved to {{ill|Matsumae Domain|en|Matsumae Domain|WD=Q18669712|lt=Matsumae Domain}} in his late teens or early twenties. He initially stayed at the home of a Matsumae retainer, Kudō Hachirōemon, while starting his business.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}} Some records indicate he may have worked as a servant for Harue Jinbē, who had already established Abe-ya in Matsumae.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in {{ill|Sōya District, Hokkaido|en|Sōya District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1185957|lt=Sōya District, Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Tomamae District, Hokkaido|en|Tomamae District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134113|lt=Tomamae District, Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Rumoi District, Hokkaido|en|Rumoi District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134204|lt=Rumoi District, Hokkaido}}, and {{ill|Ishikari District, Hokkaido|en|Ishikari District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1185986|lt=Ishikari District, Hokkaido}} under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|WD=Q101828|lt=Ainu}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: {{ill|1716|en|1716|WD=Q6892|lt=1716}} ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|1770|en|1770|WD=Q7683|lt=1770}} ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})

: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: {{ill|1738|en|1738|WD=Q6996|lt=1738}} ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era}}, he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By {{ill|1775|en|1775|WD=Q7700|lt=1775}} ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in {{ill|1778|en|1778|WD=Q7713|lt=1778}} ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the {{ill|Mashike District, Hokkaido|en|Mashike District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134336|lt=Mashike District, Hokkaido}} trade site.

: However, in {{ill|1796|en|1796|WD=Q7818|lt=1796}} ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in {{ill|1799|en|1799|WD=Q7827|lt=1799}} ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In {{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=1915}} ({{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=Taisho 3}}), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at {{ill|Hokkaidō Shrine|en|Hokkaidō Shrine|WD=Q710826|lt=Hokkaido Shrine}}'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on {{ill|Japan National Route 230|en|Japan National Route 230|WD=Q11421425|lt=National Route 230 (Japan)}}.{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:村山伝兵衛]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:村山伝兵衛]]






[[Matsumae Domain]] trader and agent {{ill|Murayama Denbee|ja|村山伝兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Denbee|qq=}} (1738–1813) is said to have founded the small shrine of {{nihongo|Sōya Daijingū|宗谷大神宮}} In {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei}} 5 (1785), enshrining [[Amaterasu]] as guardian of the north gate.<ref name="Jinjacho">{{cite web |url=https://hokkaidojinjacho.jp/北門神社/ |script-title=ja:北門神社 |trans-title=Hokumon Jinja |language=Japanese |publisher=Hokkaidō Jinja-chō |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.city.wakkanai.hokkaido.jp/gaiyo/ayumi.html |script-title=ja:稚内のあゆみ（歴史）|trans-title=History of Wakkanai |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Wakkanai, Hokkaido|Wakkanai City]] |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref> In 1896 the shrine was transferred to its current location and renamed [[Hokumon Shrine]], with [[Takemikazuchi]] and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] enshrined alongside Amaterasu.<ref name="Jinjacho"/>

==Overview==
'''Murayama Denbē''' (村山 伝兵衛) was a merchant who expanded from {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province}} to {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo}} during the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku era}}. The name "Denbē" was passed down through successive heads of the Murayama family.

The Murayama family's business was known as '''Abe-ya''' (安部屋 or 阿部屋), and their shop emblem was '''Marujūgo''' (○に十五), symbolizing the first Denbē's ambition to own 15 ships.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

== Successive Heads of the Murayama Family ==

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in {{ill|Hakui District, Ishikawa|en|Hakui District, Ishikawa|WD=Q1130953|lt=Hakui District}}, Noto Province, he moved to {{ill|Matsumae Domain|en|Matsumae Domain|WD=Q18669712|lt=Matsumae Domain}} in his late teens or early twenties. He initially stayed at the home of a Matsumae retainer, Kudō Hachirōemon, while starting his business.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}} Some records indicate he may have worked as a servant for Harue Jinbē, who had already established Abe-ya in Matsumae.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in {{ill|Sōya District, Hokkaido|en|Sōya District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1185957|lt=Sōya District, Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Tomamae District, Hokkaido|en|Tomamae District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134113|lt=Tomamae District, Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Rumoi District, Hokkaido|en|Rumoi District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134204|lt=Rumoi District, Hokkaido}}, and {{ill|Ishikari District, Hokkaido|en|Ishikari District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1185986|lt=Ishikari District, Hokkaido}} under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|WD=Q101828|lt=Ainu}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: {{ill|1716|en|1716|WD=Q6892|lt=1716}} ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|1770|en|1770|WD=Q7683|lt=1770}} ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
: The son of Shimazaki Hanbē from {{ill|Tsuruga Port|en|Tsuruga Port|WD=Q7850265|lt=Tsuruga Port}}. He worked for the Matsumae branch of the Omi merchant Manya Kiemon before being adopted as Denbē's son-in-law.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: {{ill|1738|en|1738|WD=Q6996|lt=1738}} ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era}}, he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By {{ill|1775|en|1775|WD=Q7700|lt=1775}} ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in {{ill|1778|en|1778|WD=Q7713|lt=1778}} ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the {{ill|Mashike District, Hokkaido|en|Mashike District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134336|lt=Mashike District, Hokkaido}} trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and {{ill|Machi-bugyō|en|Machi-bugyō|WD=Q908892|lt=Machi bugyō}} assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In {{ill|1789|en|1789|WD=Q7772|lt=1789}} ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the {{ill|Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|en|Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|WD=Q3454489|lt=Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion}}, the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In {{ill|1790|en|1790|WD=Q7775|lt=1790}} ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in {{ill|Sakhalin|en|Sakhalin|WD=Q7792|lt=Sakhalin}}, and by {{ill|1798|en|1798|WD=Q7823|lt=1798}} ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in {{ill|1796|en|1796|WD=Q7818|lt=1796}} ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in {{ill|1799|en|1799|WD=Q7827|lt=1799}} ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In {{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=1915}} ({{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=Taisho 3}}), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Murayama Family Genealogy ==
{{familytree/start}}
{{familytree| | | | | DE1 | | SHI | |DE1='''First Generation: Denbē'''|SHI=Shimazaki Hanbē}}
{{familytree| | | | | |!| | | |!| |}}
{{familytree| | | | | DAU |y| RIH |DAU=Daughter|RIH='''Second Generation: Rihei'''}}
{{familytree| | | | | |,|-|^|-|.| |}}
{{familytree| NIS | | BOY | | DE3 |NIS=Nishioka Jizaimon|BOY=Second Son|DE3='''Third Generation: Denbē'''}}
{{familytree/end}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at {{ill|Hokkaidō Shrine|en|Hokkaidō Shrine|WD=Q710826|lt=Hokkaido Shrine}}'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in {{ill|Minami-ku, Sapporo|en|Minami-ku, Sapporo|WD=Q1134027|lt=Minami-ku, Sapporo}} was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on {{ill|Japan National Route 230|en|Japan National Route 230|WD=Q11421425|lt=National Route 230 (Japan)}}.{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

: Born in {{ill|Hakui District, Ishikawa|en|Hakui District, Ishikawa|WD=Q1130953|lt=Hakui District|1=Hakui District, Ishikawa}}, Noto Province, he moved to {{ill|Matsumae Domain|en|Matsumae Domain|WD=Q18669712|lt=Matsumae Domain|1=Matsumae Domain}} in his late teens or early twenties. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|WD=Q101828|lt=Ainu|1=Ainu people}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and {{ill|Machi-bugyō|en|Machi-bugyō|WD=Q908892|lt=Machi bugyō|1=Machi-bugyō}} assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In {{ill|1789|en|1789|WD=Q7772|lt=1789|1=1789}} ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the {{ill|Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|en|Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|WD=Q3454489|lt=Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion|1=Menashi–Kunashir rebellion}}, the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In {{ill|1790|en|1790|WD=Q7775|lt=1790|1=1790}} ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in {{ill|Sakhalin|en|Sakhalin|WD=Q7792|lt=Sakhalin|1=Sakhalin}}, and by {{ill|1798|en|1798|WD=Q7823|lt=1798|1=1798}} ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in {{ill|1796|en|1796|WD=Q7818|lt=1796|1=1796}} ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in {{ill|1799|en|1799|WD=Q7827|lt=1799|1=1799}} ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In {{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=1915|1=1915}} ({{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=Taisho 3|1=1915}}), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}

== Legacy ==
[[File:Marujugo Bridge.JPG|thumb|Marujugo Bridge]]
Murayama Denbē is enshrined as one of the 37 deities at {{ill|Hokkaidō Shrine|en|Hokkaidō Shrine|WD=Q710826|lt=Hokkaido Shrine|1=Hokkaidō Shrine}}'s annex, {{ill|Kaitaku Shrine|ja|開拓神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitaku Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.

The area known as '''Fujinosawa''' in {{ill|Minami-ku, Sapporo|en|Minami-ku, Sapporo|WD=Q1134027|lt=Minami-ku, Sapporo|1=Minami-ku, Sapporo}} was once a logging site operated by the Murayama family. The name "Marujugo" persisted locally due to the branded marks on timber. Although the place name faded after the construction of {{ill|Fujinosawa Station|ja|藤の沢駅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujinosawa Station|1=Fujinosawa Station|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11622624}} on the {{ill|Jōzankei Railway Line|ja|定山渓鉄道線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōzankei Railway Line|1=Jōzankei Railway Line|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452195}}, the name survives in the bridge over the Okabarushi River on {{ill|Japan National Route 230|en|Japan National Route 230|WD=Q11421425|lt=National Route 230 (Japan)|1=Japan National Route 230}}.{{Sfn|Sapporo City|}}

[[Matsumae Domain]] trader and agent {{ill|Murayama Denbee|ja|村山伝兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murayama Denbee|qq=}} (1738–1813) is said to have founded the small shrine of {{nihongo|Sōya Daijingū|宗谷大神宮}} In {{ill|Tenmei|en|Tenmei|WD=Q1194121|lt=Tenmei|1=Tenmei}} 5 (1785), enshrining [[Amaterasu]] as guardian of the north gate.<ref name="Jinjacho">{{cite web |url=https://hokkaidojinjacho.jp/北門神社/ |script-title=ja:北門神社 |trans-title=Hokumon Jinja |language=Japanese |publisher=Hokkaidō Jinja-chō |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.city.wakkanai.hokkaido.jp/gaiyo/ayumi.html |script-title=ja:稚内のあゆみ（歴史）|trans-title=History of Wakkanai |language=Japanese |publisher=[[Wakkanai, Hokkaido|Wakkanai City]] |accessdate=28 September 2019}}</ref> In 1896 the shrine was transferred to its current location and renamed [[Hokumon Shrine]], with [[Takemikazuchi]] and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]] enshrined alongside Amaterasu.<ref name="Jinjacho"/>

==Overview==
'''Murayama Denbē''' (村山 伝兵衛) was a merchant who expanded from {{ill|Noto Province|en|Noto Province|WD=Q1047671|lt=Noto Province|1=Noto Province}} to {{ill|Ezo|en|Ezo|WD=Q1040184|lt=Ezo|1=Ezo}} during the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku era|1=Genroku}}. The name "Denbē" was passed down through successive heads of the Murayama family.

The Murayama family's business was known as '''Abe-ya''' (安部屋 or 阿部屋), and their shop emblem was '''Marujūgo''' (○に十五), symbolizing the first Denbē's ambition to own 15 ships.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

== Successive Heads of the Murayama Family ==

; First Generation: Murayama Denbē
: {{ill|October 5, 1683|ja|1683rd year 10月5日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 5, 1683|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Tenna 3|ja|天和3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenna 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|August 15 (Lunisolar)|ja|8月15日 (旧暦)|12=simple|lt=August 15}}) – {{ill|June 4, 1757|ja|1757th year 6月4日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/June 4, 1757|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Hōreki 7|ja|宝暦7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōreki 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|April 18 (Lunisolar)|ja|4月18日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/April 18 (Lunisolar)|lt=April 18}})
: Born in {{ill|Hakui District, Ishikawa|en|Hakui District, Ishikawa|WD=Q1130953|lt=Hakui District|1=Hakui District, Ishikawa}}, Noto Province, he moved to {{ill|Matsumae Domain|en|Matsumae Domain|WD=Q18669712|lt=Matsumae Domain|1=Matsumae Domain}} in his late teens or early twenties. He initially stayed at the home of a Matsumae retainer, Kudō Hachirōemon, while starting his business.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}} Some records indicate he may have worked as a servant for Harue Jinbē, who had already established Abe-ya in Matsumae.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: To gain residency in Matsumae, he became the adopted son of Furuya Kanzayemon, a ship captain serving the Matsumae Domain. He then established a shop in the castle town. Though he never reached 15 ships, he owned five, and he was entrusted with managing trade sites in {{ill|Sōya District, Hokkaido|en|Sōya District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1185957|lt=Sōya District, Hokkaido|1=Sōya District, Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Tomamae District, Hokkaido|en|Tomamae District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134113|lt=Tomamae District, Hokkaido|1=Tomamae District, Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Rumoi District, Hokkaido|en|Rumoi District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134204|lt=Rumoi District, Hokkaido|1=Rumoi District, Hokkaido}}, and {{ill|Ishikari District, Hokkaido|en|Ishikari District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1185986|lt=Ishikari District, Hokkaido|1=Ishikari District, Hokkaido}} under the domain's direct control. He is also credited with teaching fishing techniques to the {{ill|Ainu people|en|Ainu people|WD=Q101828|lt=Ainu|1=Ainu people}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}

; Second Generation: Murayama Rihei
: {{ill|1716|en|1716|WD=Q6892|lt=1716|1=1716}} ({{ill|Kyōhō 1|ja|享保元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|1770|en|1770|WD=Q7683|lt=1770|1=1770}} ({{ill|Meiwa 7|ja|明和7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiwa 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
: The son of Shimazaki Hanbē from {{ill|Tsuruga Port|en|Tsuruga Port|WD=Q7850265|lt=Tsuruga Port|1=Tsuruga Port}}. He worked for the Matsumae branch of the Omi merchant Manya Kiemon before being adopted as Denbē's son-in-law.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=79}}
: Though he had two children, he later left the Murayama family for unknown reasons and died at the home of Yamaguchi-ya Ihei in {{ill|Osaka|ja|大阪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Osaka|1=Ōsaka|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11439906}}.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

; Third Generation: Murayama Denbē (Hyōemon)
: {{ill|1738|en|1738|WD=Q6996|lt=1738|1=1738}} ({{ill|Genbun 3|ja|元文3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) – {{ill|February 8, 1813|ja|1813rd year 2月8日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/February 8, 1813|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Bunka 10|ja|文化10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunka 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{ill|1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|ja|1月8日 (旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st month, 8th day (lunar calendar)|qq=}})
: After his grandfather's death, he inherited the family business. During the {{ill|Meiwa|en|Meiwa|WD=Q1146161|lt=Meiwa era|1=Meiwa}}, he took over part of the Ishikari trade site. By {{ill|1775|en|1775|WD=Q7700|lt=1775|1=1775}} ({{ill|An'ei 4|ja|安永4年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 4|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he controlled the Sōya trade site, and in {{ill|1778|en|1778|WD=Q7713|lt=1778|1=1778}} ({{ill|An'ei 7|ja|安永7年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/An'ei 7|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he acquired the {{ill|Mashike District, Hokkaido|en|Mashike District, Hokkaido|WD=Q1134336|lt=Mashike District, Hokkaido|1=Mashike District, Hokkaido}} trade site.
: His success led to permission for {{ill|samurai|ja|苗字帯刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/samurai|1=myoujitaitou|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11616116}}, and he also served as {{ill|Machi toshiyori|ja|町年寄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Machi toshiyori|1=machidoshiyori|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11578236}} and {{ill|Machi-bugyō|en|Machi-bugyō|WD=Q908892|lt=Machi bugyō|1=Machi-bugyō}} assistant, becoming a key figure in the region.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=80}}

: In {{ill|1789|en|1789|WD=Q7772|lt=1789|1=1789}} ({{ill|Kansei 1|ja|寛政元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), following the {{ill|Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|en|Menashi–Kunashir rebellion|WD=Q3454489|lt=Kunashir-Menashi Rebellion|1=Menashi–Kunashir rebellion}}, the Matsumae Domain removed Hidaya Kyūbē from his trade rights in Eastern Ezo and assigned Denbē to oversee relief efforts for the Ainu.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=81}}

: In {{ill|1790|en|1790|WD=Q7775|lt=1790|1=1790}} ({{ill|Kansei 2|ja|寛政2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he launched an expedition to develop a trading site in {{ill|Sakhalin|en|Sakhalin|WD=Q7792|lt=Sakhalin|1=Sakhalin}}, and by {{ill|1798|en|1798|WD=Q7823|lt=1798|1=1798}} ({{ill|Kansei 10|ja|寛政10年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 10|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was hailed as the "Eastern Yokozuna" alongside {{ill|Kōnoike Zen'emon|ja|鴻池善右衛門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnoike Zen'emon|1=Kōnoike Zen-emon|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11173943}} in the national wealth Rankings.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=82}}

: However, in {{ill|1796|en|1796|WD=Q7818|lt=1796|1=1796}} ({{ill|Kansei 8|ja|寛政8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), he was abruptly removed from his trade rights due to political maneuvering by Osaka merchant Koyama Gonbē.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=83}} His fortunes were later restored, and in {{ill|1799|en|1799|WD=Q7827|lt=1799|1=1799}} ({{ill|Kansei 11|ja|寛政11年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansei 11|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), the Tokugawa Shogunate made him an official government trade manager in Ezo.{{Sfn|Itō|2008|p=84}}

: In {{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=1915|1=1915}} ({{ill|1915|en|1915|WD=Q2084|lt=Taisho 3|1=1915}}), he was posthumously awarded the {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|1=Junior Fifth Rank|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank|qq=}}.{{Sfn|Tajiri|1975|p=39}}
